refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication latency with p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions rather than optimistic average-case assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit latency budgeting across the communication chain.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (command input to sensor feedback display) under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Collect p95 percentile over minimum 1000 round-trip cycles per test scenario. Validate against 150 ms threshold with safety margin analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode vehicle state telemetry with latency ≤ 30 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit encoded telemetry over network link with latency ≤ 80 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and decode operator commands with latency ≤ 20 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall apply decoded commands to vehicle actuators with latency ≤ 20 ms.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency teleoperation architecture with hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding, dedicated network priority queuing, and direct sensor-to-network data paths to minimize processing hops.
DP-B: Deploy edge computing nodes at vehicle and operator stations with local state caching and predictive command buffering to reduce round-trip dependency.

DP-C: Establish latency budget allocation across telemetry acquisition (30 ms), network transmission (80 ms), command reception (20 ms), and actuation (20 ms) with per-component monitoring and adaptive quality-of-service throttling.
DP-D: Use commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) teleoperation middleware with certified latency profiles and fallback to degraded-mode operation if latency exceeds threshold.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Establish explicit latency budget allocation (30 ms telemetry acquisition + 80 ms network transmission + 20 ms command reception + 20 ms actuation = 150 ms total) with per-component monitoring, adaptive QoS, and safety-critical fallback mechanisms.

potential_sources: Network link performance (DP-C depends on Network Link interface quality; coupling to external infrastructure)
Sensor/actuator hardware capabilities (DP-C assumes vehicle hardware can meet 30 ms + 20 ms bounds; coupling to Vehicle module)
Safety Services integration (DP-C requires SafetyAlert feedback loop; potential coupling if safety response latency not budgeted separately)
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is decomposable and does not create circular dependencies. Coupling is primarily to external interfaces (Network Link, Vehicle hardware) and Safety Services, which are manageable via interface contracts and latency SLAs. No internal architectural coupling detected.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network link can sustain p95 ≤ 80 ms latency under agreed conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss). RISK: If network infrastructure is shared or congested, this assumption may not hold. Mitigation: Establish network SLA with QoS guarantees; implement adaptive bitrate control.

ASSUMPTION: Vehicle telemetry sensors and actuators can meet 30 ms + 20 ms latency bounds. RISK: Legacy or slow sensors/actuators may exceed budget. Mitigation: Conduct hardware capability assessment; consider sensor fusion or predictive filtering to mask latency.

ASSUMPTION: Safety Services can respond to latency degradation (e.g., trigger safe shutdown) within acceptable time. RISK: If SafetyAlert feedback loop is slow, safety margin is eroded. Mitigation: Allocate separate safety response budget; validate SafetyAlert latency independently.

ASSUMPTION: Operator and site network conditions match 'agreed network conditions' during deployment. RISK: Field conditions may differ (e.g., poor signal, interference). Mitigation: Implement adaptive mode selection; provide operator training on latency-aware operation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly separate the latency budget into two tiers: (1) nominal operation (p95 ≤ 150 ms) and (2) degraded-mode operation (p95 ≤ 250 ms with reduced command frequency or sensor resolution). This provides a safety margin and allows graceful degradation if network conditions worsen. Additionally, specify that per-component monitoring must include real-time latency telemetry visible to the operator and Safety Manager for situational awareness.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the end-to-end teleoperation latency budget, including command reception, telemetry encoding/transmission, and actuation feedback loops. Responsible for latency monitoring, adaptive QoS, and coordination with Vehicle and Safety Services modules to meet p95 ≤ 150 ms constraint.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs encoded vehicle state telemetry (position, orientation, sensor readings, operational status) to operator display and Safety Services. Latency budget: ≤ 30 ms acquisition + ≤ 80 ms transmission.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) from remote operator station. Latency budget: ≤ 20 ms reception + ≤ 20 ms actuation application.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module exchanges safety-critical signals with Safety Services: receives SafetyAlert notifications (e.g., latency threshold exceeded, unsafe condition detected) and sends latency telemetry for safety monitoring. Latency budget: ≤ 50 ms round-trip for safety-critical feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with network infrastructure for bidirectional command/telemetry transmission. Assumes network SLA: p95 ≤ 80 ms latency, configurable QoS priority, packet loss < 1%.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of latency budget allocation, monitoring, and adaptive QoS. All teleoperation-specific logic (encoding, command reception, feedback loops) is contained. Minor diffusion: dependency on Network Link SLA and Vehicle hardware capabilities (external to module).

Vehicle module owns sensor/actuator hardware but does not own teleoperation latency budget or network communication logic. Assigning IM to Vehicle would scatter teleoperation concerns across hardware and software layers, violating cohesion. Vehicle should provide latency-compliant sensor/actuator interfaces to Teleoperation.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and alert logic, not teleoperation latency management. Assigning IM to Safety Services would conflate safety oversight with operational latency control. Safety Services should consume Telemetry and Tasking interfaces to monitor latency compliance, not own the latency budget.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would avoid architectural decomposition and leave latency responsibility undefined. Teleoperation is a subsystem that should be owned by a dedicated module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the appropriate owner of CR-NGHE-008 because it encapsulates the end-to-end latency budget, command/telemetry processing, and adaptive QoS logic. External dependencies (Network Link SLA, Vehicle hardware latency) are managed via interface contracts, not internal to the module.
containment_risks: RISK: If Network Link interface does not meet p95 ≤ 80 ms SLA, Teleoperation cannot meet overall p95 ≤ 150 ms target. MITIGATION: Establish network SLA as a hard constraint; implement fallback to degraded-mode operation if network latency exceeds threshold.

RISK: If Vehicle module cannot provide sensor/actuator latency ≤ 30 ms + 20 ms, Teleoperation budget is exceeded. MITIGATION: Conduct Vehicle hardware assessment early; allocate additional latency margin if needed; consider sensor fusion or predictive filtering.

RISK: If Safety Services SafetyAlert feedback loop is slow (> 50 ms), safety margin is eroded and operator may not receive timely warnings. MITIGATION: Allocate separate safety response budget; validate SafetyAlert latency independently; ensure operator training on latency-aware operation.

RISK: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if it attempts to centralize all latency monitoring and QoS logic. MITIGATION: Distribute monitoring across Vehicle and Safety Services via interface telemetry; use asynchronous event-driven architecture to avoid blocking.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the interface contract for Network Link: specify that the network infrastructure must guarantee p95 ≤ 80 ms latency under agreed conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Define fallback behavior if Network Link SLA is violated (e.g., reduce command frequency, trigger SafetyAlert, enter degraded-mode operation). Additionally, establish a latency monitoring dashboard visible to the Operator and Safety Manager, implemented as a Telemetry sub-interface, to provide real-time visibility into latency budget consumption.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (explicit latency budget allocation with per-component monitoring and adaptive QoS) directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 (end-to-end teleoperation latency p95 ≤ 150 ms). The DP is decomposable into sub-DPs for each latency component (telemetry acquisition, network transmission, command reception, actuation). No coupling or contradiction detected. DP-C is implementable and verifiable.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module depends on Network Link interface SLA (p95 ≤ 80 ms). If network infrastructure is not contractually bound to this SLA, latency target is at risk. MITIGATION: Establish network SLA as a hard constraint in system requirements.

Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle module to provide sensor/actuator latency ≤ 30 ms + 20 ms. If Vehicle hardware cannot meet this, Teleoperation budget is exceeded. MITIGATION: Conduct Vehicle hardware assessment; allocate additional margin if needed.

Teleoperation module interfaces with Safety Services via SafetyAlert. If SafetyAlert feedback loop is slow, safety margin is eroded. MITIGATION: Allocate separate safety response budget; validate SafetyAlert latency independently.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the appropriate owner of latency budget allocation and monitoring. External dependencies are managed via interface contracts (Network Link SLA, Vehicle hardware latency, SafetyAlert response time). No internal architectural diffusion detected. Module boundary is clear and cohesive.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via end-to-end latency measurement under representative network conditions. Measure command input to sensor feedback display latency; collect p95 percentile over minimum 1000 round-trip cycles. Validate against 150 ms threshold with safety margin analysis. Teleoperation module can provide latency telemetry via Telemetry interface for real-time monitoring.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns latency budget allocation (30 ms + 80 ms + 20 ms + 20 ms = 150 ms) and per-component monitoring. Vehicle module owns sensor/actuator hardware latency (30 ms + 20 ms). Safety Services owns safety-critical latency monitoring and alert logic. Network Link interface owns network transmission latency (80 ms). No ambiguity or overlap detected.

recommended_revisions: Establish network SLA as a hard constraint: Network Link interface must guarantee p95 ≤ 80 ms latency under agreed conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Define fallback behavior if SLA is violated.
Conduct Vehicle hardware assessment to confirm sensor/actuator latency ≤ 30 ms + 20 ms. If not achievable, allocate additional latency margin or implement sensor fusion/predictive filtering.
Define SafetyAlert latency budget separately (≤ 50 ms round-trip) to ensure safety margin is not eroded by slow safety feedback.
Implement latency monitoring dashboard visible to Operator and Safety Manager via Telemetry interface. Provide real-time visibility into latency budget consumption and adaptive QoS state.

Specify degraded-mode operation: if latency exceeds p95 ≤ 150 ms, reduce command frequency or sensor resolution to maintain safety margin. Define operator training requirements for latency-aware operation.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs to allocate latency budget across telemetry acquisition, network transmission, command reception, and actuation. DP-C (explicit latency budget allocation) is sound and passes FR-DP gate. Teleoperation module is the appropriate IM owner and passes DP-IM gate. However, the refined FR identifies four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through FR-NGHE-008.4) that must be decomposed further to define specific DPs and IMs for each latency component. Additionally, external dependencies (Network Link SLA, Vehicle hardware latency, SafetyAlert response time) must be validated before proceeding to detailed design. Recommend decomposing to next level to allocate latency budget and validate interface contracts.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode vehicle state telemetry with latency ≤ 30 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit encoded telemetry over network link with latency ≤ 80 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and decode operator commands with latency ≤ 20 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall apply decoded commands to vehicle actuators with latency ≤ 20 ms.

assumptions: Network Link interface can sustain p95 ≤ 80 ms latency under agreed conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles).
Vehicle module can provide sensor/actuator latency ≤ 30 ms + 20 ms.
Safety Services SafetyAlert feedback loop can respond within ≤ 50 ms round-trip.
Operator and site network conditions match 'agreed network conditions' during deployment.
Teleoperation module can implement adaptive QoS and fallback to degraded-mode operation if latency exceeds threshold.
open_risks: Network infrastructure may not meet p95 ≤ 80 ms SLA if shared or congested. Mitigation: Establish network SLA with QoS guarantees; implement adaptive bitrate control.
Vehicle hardware may not meet 30 ms + 20 ms latency bounds if sensors/actuators are slow. Mitigation: Conduct hardware assessment; consider sensor fusion or predictive filtering.
SafetyAlert feedback loop may be slow, eroding safety margin. Mitigation: Allocate separate safety response budget; validate SafetyAlert latency independently.
Field network conditions may differ from 'agreed network conditions', causing latency degradation. Mitigation: Implement adaptive mode selection; provide operator training on latency-aware operation.

Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if it centralizes all latency monitoring and QoS logic. Mitigation: Distribute monitoring across Vehicle and Safety Services; use asynchronous event-driven architecture.
evidence_needed: Network SLA contract specifying p95 ≤ 80 ms latency under agreed conditions.
Vehicle hardware latency assessment confirming sensor/actuator latency ≤ 30 ms + 20 ms.
SafetyAlert latency specification and validation (≤ 50 ms round-trip).
End-to-end latency measurement under representative network conditions (minimum 1000 round-trip cycles per test scenario).
Operator training plan for latency-aware operation and degraded-mode fallback.
Latency monitoring dashboard design and implementation plan.
Adaptive QoS and fallback behavior specification for Teleoperation module.
